front: What is the granularity of data items in DBMS?
back: Granularity refers to the size or level of detail at which locks are acquired in a database system. It determines what portion of the database (database, file, page, record, or field) is locked when a transaction accesses data.

front: What are the different levels of data granularity from coarse to fine?
back: The hierarchy is: Database → File → Page → Record → Field. Coarse granularity (e.g., entire database) offers less concurrency but low overhead; fine granularity (e.g., individual record) offers high concurrency but higher overhead.

MGL is a locking technique that allows locks to be acquired at different levels of granularity simultaneously. It uses intent locks to indicate that a transaction holds locks at lower levels, enabling efficient lock management in a hierarchical structure.

front: What are the six lock modes in multiple granularity locking?
back: The six lock modes are: IS (Intent Shared), IX (Intent Exclusive), S (Shared), SIX (Shared with Intent Exclusive), X (Exclusive), and NL (No Lock). Each has different compatibility with other locks.

front: What is the purpose of Intent locks (IS and IX)?
back: Intent locks indicate that a transaction plans to lock finer-grained resources at a lower level. IS indicates intention to read lower-level items; IX indicates intention to read and write lower-level items. They help in efficient lock escalation and prevention of deadlocks.
An S lock is acquired when a transaction needs to read data. Multiple transactions can hold S locks on the same item simultaneously because reading does not conflict with other reads.
An X lock is acquired when a transaction needs to both read and write (modify) data. Only one transaction can hold an X lock on an item at a time, ensuring no other transaction can read or write.

front: What is a SIX lock and when is it used?
back: SIX (Shared with Intent Exclusive) lock is held when a transaction has an S lock on a node and also plans to acquire X locks on some descendants. It allows reading the entire subtree while modifying specific records.

A table that shows which lock modes can be granted simultaneously. Rows represent the existing lock, columns represent the requested lock. For example, S is compatible with S and IS but not with X or IX.

front: What are advantages of coarse granularity locking?
back: Advantages: 1) Less memory for lock management, 2) Fewer lock operations needed, 3) Lower CPU overhead, 4) Faster lock acquisition and release, 5) Better for operations accessing many items.

front: What are advantages of fine granularity locking?
back: Advantages: 1) Higher degree of concurrency, 2) More transactions can run simultaneously, 3) Better for transactions accessing few items, 4) Reduced lock contention.

Lock escalation is the process of converting many fine-grained locks into fewer coarse-grained locks when the number of locks held by a transaction exceeds a threshold. It reduces lock management overhead.

front: What is a lock hierarchy tree in MGL?
back: A hierarchical tree structure showing different granularity levels (database → file → page → record). Locks are acquired from root to leaf, and released from leaf to root following a two-phase locking approach.

front: Why are intent locks necessary in multiple granularity locking?
back: Intent locks avoid unnecessary scanning of the entire tree when checking lock compatibility. They signal that lower-level locks exist, allowing efficient conflict detection without examining each descendant.

front: What is the order of lock acquisition in multiple granularity locking?
back: Locks are acquired in a top-down manner (root to leaf) following the hierarchy. When moving down the tree, if the parent has an S or X lock, certain rules apply. Locks are released in bottom-up order (leaf to root) during the unlocking phase.
